20. Stochastic Approximation (MC?)

A variety of approximation tools are available. Roughly speaking, they can be categorized into three categories: stochastic approximation, asymptotic approximation, and variational approximation. We will explore the key ideas behind each of these in this and subsequent sections of these notes, developing the required analysis tools along the way.

Assume we want to approximate sampling from a distribution using a distribution . For both distributions, we need not assume that and are computatoinally feasible to evaluate, but rather we can compute unnormalized forms (so that ) and .

Stochastic = monte carlo methods. Example problem 7.3

20.3. MCMC

MCMC methods asymptotically produce correlated samples from by constructing an ergodic Markov chain whose stationary distribution is the target distribution . It avoids the problem encountered with rejection sampling that the proposal distribution needs to be in any sense “close” to the target distribution .

20.3.1. Homogenous Markov Chains

Homogenous = Time homogenous, i.e. transition probabilities don’t change with time.

Definition 20.1 (Finite-State Homogeneous Markov Chain). A sequence of variables , each taking values in forms a finite-state homogeneous Markov Chain (FSHMC) if

  1. is finite
  2. For every we have the Markov chain
  3. The Collection of distributions does not depend on .

Definition 20.2 (Stationary Distribution). A distribution is a stationary distribution of a FSHMC if it satisfies the global balance equations:

(20.3)

Definition 20.3 (Fully Communicating Chain). A FSHMC is fully communicating25 if it possible (with positive probability) to go from any state to any other state (including itself) within some (finite) number of time steps, i.e., for every , there exists such that .
Theorem 20.1 (Consequence of Perron-Frobenius). If a FSHMC is fully communicating, then it has a unique stationary distribution.
Definition 20.4 (Aperiodic Chain). A fully communicating FSHMC is aperiodic if it can return to an initial state at any desired time sufficiently far in the future, i.e. there exists such that for all and some we have .

Remark. This is a consequence of the definition of periodicity in 6.77.

Theorem 20.2 (FTOMC). If a fully communicating FSHMC with stationary distribution is aperiodic, then for any choice of , we have

(20.4)

Definition 20.5 (Reversible FSHMC). A fully communicating FSHMC with stationary distribution is reversible if the chain looks the same probabilistically whether time runs forwards or backwards:

(20.5)

Proposition 20.1 (Detailed Balance). A fully communicating FSHMC is reversible if and only if its stationary distribution satisfies the detailed balance conditions

(20.6)

20.3.2. MCMC Approximations

Since KL divergence is continuous in the second argument, we obtain from the FTOMC that but we know nothing about its rate of convergence (governed by the mixing time of the chain—short mixing times correspond to fast convergence).

Theorem 20.3. The second largest eigenvalue of the transition matrix characterizes the mixing time.

Remarks

  • The first eigenvalue and corresponds to the stationary state.
  • Proof is out of scope but we can prove the next result

20.3.3. MH Algorithm

The self transition property is specified by 1 minus the probability of transitioning to another state.

Theorem 20.4 (Metropolis-Hastings MCMC). Given a finite statespace and proposal transition distribution , the sequence of samples generated by the MH algorithm correspond to a sequence of random variables that form a reversible Markov chain with transition distribution

(20.8)

for which the target is a stationary distribution.

Proof

Use detailed balance equations wrt to show that its reversible with stationary distribution .

□

20.3.4. Correlation Time

MCMC methods produce correlated samples. The number of effectively independent samples in the output scales inversely with the correlation time of the chain.

MH effectively minimizes one measure of this corelation within a class of MCMC methods. [TODO] It turns out MH yields the maximum acceptance rate.

20.3.5. Burn-in time

Depends on mixing time and the quality of approximation being sought.

Difficult to estimate mixing time, so we take an empirical approach.

20.3.6. Proposal distribution requirements

Corollary 20.1: Applying FTOMC (Theorem 20.2) to the MH algorithm (Theorem 20.4) yields that if the proposal distribution is such that the induced Markov chain is fully comunicating and aperiodic, then will have a distribution approaching the target as .

If is fully communicating and for all distinct pairs , then the Markov chain induced by the MH algorithm is fully communicating.

Corollary 20.2: For positive (nowhere zero) target distributions, the equivalent condition is if and only if for all distinct .

Corollary 20.3: If COrollary 20.2 is satisifed and for at least one distinct pair , then the induced Markov chain is both fully communicating and aperiodic.

20.4. Rejection Sampling Perspective on MH*

Consider specializing MH to the case where the proposal transition distribution is a Markov chain in which the samples are i.i.d., i.e. the transition probability does not depend on the current state . Then we acept a sample generated from with probability from our development of rejection sampling. is chosen as a global constant, which is the root of the inefficiency of conventional rejection sampling.

MH can be viewed as a local version of rejection sampling. To see this, consider replacing with a more general proposal distribution and applying MH with the global rejection sampling acceptance factor . Then it follows immediately that detailed balance is satisfied with this acceptance factor:

This chain will produce samples from the desired distribution, but the rate will be low because must be large enough to meet the global constraint.

From this perspective, the key insight of MH is that it is not necessary to choose the acceptance rate to satisfy the global constraint; it need only satisfy a local one corresponding to detaild balance. Smaller chooses means a higher acceptance rate, so we choose the smallest such that it still yields a valid probability (this ends up as taking the maximum of two numbers26)
